title: Methylphenidate Effects in Learning Disabilities
abstract:
Methylphenidate Effects in Learning
Disabilities
Psychometric Changes
Rachel Gittelman-Klein, PhD, Donald F. Klein, MD
\s=b\Sixty-one children of average intelligence with appreciable
learning lags, but no behavior disorders, received placebo or
methylphenidate hydrochloride for a 12-week period. Methyl-
phenidate was instrumental in improving performance on many
psychological tests, but did not affect performance on standard-
ized achievement tests.
None of the patient characteristics
investigated was strongly predictive of drug effect.
Methylphenidate seems to have a specific effect on visual-
motor processes, which in turn positively affect performance
tasks, but not verbal tasks. Under the conditions of this study,
methylphenidate treatment alone did not emerge as a useful
agent for the amelioration of reading performance, although the
data provide evidence for stimulant effects on children's cogni-
tive functions.
(Arch Gen Psychiatry 33:655-664, 1976)
The
stimulants have the longest history of all major
psychopharmacologic agents in child psychiatry. They
were originally used by Bradley, in the 1930s, in the hope of
improving the school performance of children in a residen¬
tial treatment center, since stimulants improved adults'
performance on intelligence and other psychological tests.
Serendipitously, Bradley noted that some of the children in
this
mixed
psychopathologic
group
became
calmer.
Relatively well-designed studies established the fact that
over a short period of time, stimulants reduced distracti-
bility and hyperactivity. In addition, performance
on
various psychological tests was improved by stimulants
among mixed groups of "emotionally disturbed"
and
hyperactive children, with and without learning difficul¬
ties. Studies are somewhat inconsistent in the specific tests
that show improved results with treatment; the most
prominent are the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children
(WISC) performance, intelligence quotient (IQ), Porteus
mazes, and reaction time. Since published reports are too
numerous
to list,
reference
is
made
to
a literature
review.1
All studies so far have reported on the performance of
children selected for behavior disorders, conduct problems,
or hyperactivity, sometimes combined with children with
learning disabilities. In poor learners with notable psycho-
pathologic findings, it is difficult to determine whether
learning difficulties are secondary to adjustment prob¬
lems, or whether these problems coexist independently. If
the learning lags were secondary to the interpersonal
maladjustment, improved behavior might lead to improved
academic performance. Since no study of stimulant activ¬
ity among children without psychopathologic findings has
been conducted, it is not known whether the reported
improvement in test performance represents a primary
stimulant drug effect on cognitive processes, or a second¬
ary improvement due to a primary, drug-induced, behav¬
ioral improvement.
This consideration has both practical and theoretical
importance due to the spreading belief that minimal brain
dysfunction (MBD) occurs in a variety of childhood disor¬
ders.
Within the MBD category, both hyperkinetic children
and children with specific learning lags have been included.
Further, MBD has been considered by some to be a syn¬
drome for which a specific treatment is indicated, namely,
stimulants (ie, dextroamphetamine sulfate, methylpheni¬
date
hydrochloride,
and,
more
recently,
magnesium
Accepted for publication March 12, 1975.
From the Psychology Department, Queens College, City University of
New York, Flushing, NY (Dr Gittelman-Klein), and the Psychiatry Depart-
ment, Long Island Jewish-Hillside Medical Center, Glen Oaks, NY (Drs
Gittelman-Klein and Klein).
Reprint requests to Child Development Clinic, Long Island Jewish-
Hillside Medical Center, Hillside Division, Glen Oaks, NY
11004 (Dr
Gittelman-Klein).
Downloaded From: http://archpsyc.jamanetwork.com/pdfaccess.ashx?url=/data/journals/psych/12274/ by a University of California - San Diego User  on 02/01/2017
 pemoline).2 The primary indication for methylphenidate
therapy in children, as stated in the package insert, is for
"management in the treatment of minimal brain dysfunc¬
tion in children." It is then added that hyperkinetic
behavior is a frequent manifestation of MBD. However,
the primary target for the use of the drug is the diagnostic
construct of MBD, rather than
a behavioral syndrome.
Therefore, there is the implicit assumption that stimulant
therapy may be a relevant intervention in children with
MBD who have a variety of difficulties. This would include
children who only manifest specific learning disabilities,
since their academic lags may result from cognitive defi¬
cits caused by subtle central nervous system developmental
dysfunction.3 However, no empirical justification exists, as
yet, for the practice of treating such MBD children with
stimulants, without concern for initial behavioral symp¬
toms.
This study was designed to test whether methylpheni¬
date therapy improves the cognitive performance and
academic achievement of children with learning deficits,
but without appreciable behavior problems. The study was
conducted over the three-year period, 1970 to 1973.
SUBJECTS AND METHODS
Learning disability
was operationally defined
as being two
years below reading grade level despite average intelligence.
To qualify for the study, children between the ages of 7 and 13
had to fulfill the following criteria: (1) be attending a normal
school; (2) be referred to a research children's clinic because of
learning problems; (3) have one WISC sub-IQ of 85 or more, an
overall IQ of at least 80; (4) be two years below grade level in
reading either on the Wide-Range Achievement Test (WRAT) or
the Gray Oral Reading Test; (5) be evaluated as being free of
cròss-situational hyperactivity; (6) be free of appreciable psycho-
pathologic findings; and (7) be in school through the duration of
the study. Other criteria consisted of the family having a tele¬
phone, parents being fluent in English, and English being spoken
at home.
After school referral, children were tested for IQ and achieve¬
ment. If the criteria for IQ and reading level specified above were
met, a teacher's school report form4 was sent to the school and an
interview scheduled for the mother to meet with a social worker.
Parents were informed of the investigational nature of the treat¬
ment, as well as of the use of a placebo. They were further told that
we had no knowledge of whether the drug would be helpful to their
child.
Both the mother and social worker filled out a behavior check¬
list'1 designed to indicate the level of the child's motor activity at
home. In addition, a rating of the current family situation was
made by the social worker on a form reflecting the degree of
stability and nurturance in the home (no validity or reliability data
are available for this rating).
Hyperactivity in the classroom was evaluated by means of the
teacher's school report form. Items reflecting hyperactivity were
selected as criteria. Eleven items on the mother's behavior check¬
list and the social worker's that unequivocally indicate motor
activity were selected to estimate activity level in the home.
Each child received a psychiatric diagnostic evaluation following
usual clinical practice; a review was done of preschool and school
history, and the child as well as his mother were interviewed. The
psychiatrist filled out
a Children's Psychiatric Rating Scale,"
recording the child's level of motor activity, his affect, mood,
anxious concerns, and speech. The criteria for the DSM II diag¬
nosis of specific learning disability had to be met. In addition to
the psychiatric rating, level of motor activity in the clinic was
rated by the psychologist during IQ and achievement testing on a
form devised for this purpose. Children reported to be hyperactive
in school were excluded; however, if the mother rated the child as
hyperactive, but the teacher and clinic staff did not, the child was
accepted.
If the child met the criteria, and electroencephalogram (EEG),
clinical neurological examination,
and
further psychological
testing were performed. The tests consisted of the Porteus mazes,
Beery Developmental Test of Visual-Motor Integration,7 Draw-A-
Person, the Visual Sequential Memory Test, a Paired Associate
Test, a Continuous Performance Test, and a test of hand, eye, and
foot laterality. Hand laterality was ascertained with a cutting and
writing task. Eye dominance was measured by the child's looking
through a pinhole and kaleidoscope; foot dominance was measured
by the child's kicking a ball and standing on one foot.
Overall laterality represents a combined rating of hand, eye, and
foot laterality. A four-point scale was used: 3, the three modes are
consistent; 2, hand dominance is the same as either eye or foot
dominance; 1, eye and foot have the same dominance, hand differs;
and 0, there is no agreement among eye, hand, and foot domi¬
nance, or two of the three organs do not have clearly established
dominance.
The fact that a child was currently receiving academic remedial
assistance, or had received such assistance in the past, did not
preclude entry in the study.
The children were randomly assigned to placebo and methyl¬
phenidate treatment for a 12-week period on a double-blind basis.
Dosage of methylphenidate hydrochloride was fixed for the first
four weeks; the goal was for each child to reach, if possible, a daily
dose of 60 mg/day administered in two 30-mg doses (week 1,10 mg
twice daily; week 2, 20 mg twice daily; week 3, 30 mg twice daily).
After four weeks of treatment, the child continued on the best-
tolerated dose. The child was seen weekly by a psychiatrist for
four weeks and biweekly for the following eight weeks. Though a
relationship often developed between the doctor and child, no
psychotherapy-in the traditional
sense—was undertaken. The
battery of psychological tests was repeated 4 and 12 weeks after
treatment. At the same intervals, parents and teachers filled out
behavior rating scales.
Differences in test performance between the placebo and drug
groups were analyzed by means of covariance analyses; postad¬
ministration scores were corrected for baseline values. Since a
positive drug effect was predicted on the psychological tests, one-
tailed tests of significance were applied. In addition, the slope of
each group was computed (fe), as well as F tests of the difference
between b values. Whenever the F test indicates a significant
slope difference between the groups, the conditions for analysis of
covariance
are not met and such
a procedure is technically
incorrect. Nevertheless, analyses of covariance were performed in
such cases, since the results represent conservative estimates of
group differences.
RESULTS
Subjects
Sixty-four children entered the study. Three children did not
complete the 12-week trial; two were sensitive to the medication,
with side effects developing on as little as 5 mg/day. One subject
left the study before completion. The total sample consisted of 61
subjects:
32 receiving placebo and 29, methylphenidate. The
average age was 10 years, 9 months. There were 47 boys and 14
girls, 40 white and 21 black children. The socioeconomic status
(SES) of the families, as reflected by the two-factor determina¬
tion, tended to be low; over half the children came from classes 4
and 5. The demographic characteristics are presented in Table 1.
(One child in the placebo group failed to take the medication for

Sixty-four children entered the study. Three children did not
complete the 12-week trial; two were sensitive to the medication,
with side effects developing on as little as 5 mg/day. One subject
left the study before completion. The total sample consisted of 61
32 receiving placebo and 29, methylphenidate. The
average age was 10 years, 9 months. There were 47 boys and 14
girls, 40 white and 21 black children. The socioeconomic status
(SES) of the families, as reflected by the two-factor determina¬
tion, tended to be low; over half the children came from classes 4
and 5. The demographic characteristics are presented in Table 1.
(One child in the placebo group failed to take the medication for
Table 1.—Demographic and Baseline Characteristics
several days prior to the final réévaluation. Without knowledge of
the treatment, it was decided not to test him.)
The Family Stability Scale is scored from
rating represents the best possible score. The sample mean of 1.46
= .64) indicates that, on the whole, children came from stable
home environments, where adequate nurturance was available.
Eighty-four percent of the children were right-handed (51 of 61);
seven were left-handed, and three had mixed hand dominance.
A total of 51 of the 61 subjects had received some sort of
academic remediation in their school. Of these, 25 received methyl¬
phenidate and 26, placebo. Therefore, the sample consisted of
children who were lagging appreciably in academic achievement,
in spite of previous remedial efforts.
No differences between the two treatment groups were found at
baseline in EEG abnormality and number of neurological signs.
These data, as well as the behavioral ratings, will be presented
Psychometric Characteristics.-Table 2 presents the psychometric
test results for the total sample and for each treatment group. The
average IQ for the sample was 92.26. No differences were found
between the two treatment groups with regard to any of the
demographic variables. Given the average age in the sample of 10
years 9 months, the expected reading level is at least at the fifth
grade. Therefore, the children had an average reading level more
than two years below grade on both the Gray Oral Reading Test
and the WRAT (1.95 and 2.49 grade levels, respectively), and an
average almost two years below grade in arithmetic on the WRAT
(3.38). The other arithmetic score obtained, the WISC oral arith¬
metic subtest, also indicated a lag. The group mean scale score of
7.6 reflects a lag of about l'/à years.
As expected in a group of poor readers, the children were also
lagging in spelling (WRAT grade level, 2.51). The baseline scores
presented in Table 2 are for the total sample evaluated at baseline.
were missing on retest; however, the
baseline values for the children with missing retest data were no
different from the other children. Other than a trend (P = .057)
for the methylphenidate-treated subjects to have higher Draw-A-
Person IQ scores, the two treatment groups did not differ on the
baseline psychometric measures.
The visual-motor development of the sample was also retarded;
at baseline, the average performance on the Visual Motor Integra¬
tion Test was at the 100-month-old level, indicating a maturational
The Visual Sequential Memory Test is one of the subtests of the
Illinois Test of Psycholinguistic Abilities (ITPA). The child is
presented, for a fixed period, with tiles that have printed geo¬
metric figures in sequences of increasing complexity. He is given
the tiles and asked to reproduce the arrangement. The numerical
scores obtained have age norms. The average of 22.85 obtained by
this group is at the 9-years 9-months level, about one year below
Table 2.—Baseline Means of Psychological Test Scores
Visual-Motor Integration Test, mo
* Placebo vs methylphenidate groups; two-tailed tests of significance.
Table 3.—Intercorrelations of Baseline Characteristics
¿ Wide-Range Achievement Test.
The Paired Associate Test is a test of rote visual association.
The child was shown a series of ten meaningless written designs,
each paired with a familiar object. The presentation was on a
teaching machine display, lasting five seconds for each trial. The
meaningless design was displayed again and the subject was asked
to recall the familiar object. The score obtained represents the
number of trials necessary to learn
Therefore, the lower the score, the more rapid the rote learning.
On the Paired Associate Test, the children required an average
of 6.38 trials before they learned ten consecutive associations. No
norms are available for this test of rote memory.
On the Continuous Performance Test, the child was told to press
a button when a red circle appeared in the display window of a
teaching machine. The red circle is embedded in random order
among other geometric figures that may be red or blue. Each
exposure lasts five seconds; the total test lasts ten minutes. The
red circle, ie, criterion stimulus, appeared 26 times. The child could
make two types of errors: (1) he could press the button though the
criterion stimulus had not appeared (error ofcommission) or (2) he
could fail to press the button when the criterion stimulus appeared
(error of omission). This test is designed to tap the ability to
On the Continuous Performance Test, the children pressed the
button an average of 1.22 times when the criterion stimulus was
not displayed, which is a very low level of errors of commission.
Failure to press the button when the criterion stimulus appeared
(errors of omission) occurred less than once on the average (.65
times). These results indicate that the test was too easy for the
children, and that the ceiling of their attention-sustaining ability
Intercorrelation of Baseline Variables
To determine to what extent the sample characteristics were
associated, the baseline variables were intercorrelated. The results
are presented in Tables 3 and 4 for the placebo and methylpheni¬
date groups, respectively. As expected, the older children tended to
have higher scores on the achievement tests (P < .05). Further,
performance on the Gray Oral Reading and WRAT reading tests
were significantly correlated (r = .79 and .77;  < .01).
Some differences were found in the groups' composition. Given
a multitude of characteristics, random assignment may
prevent occasional specific patterns from piling up unequally. In
the placebo group, the black children tended to be older than the
white children (r = .45;  = .01); the opposite relationship (r =
— .43;  < .05) was found in the drug group. In the placebo group,
the black children tended to come from more unstable homes
(r = .49;  < .01). The fact that in the methylphenidate group,
white children tended to have higher scores in arithmetic (r =
- .43;  < .05), is most likely due to the fact that the black children
In both groups, the children's IQs were positively associated
with arithmetic performance on the WRAT.
The children's socioeconomic levels and family stability were not
associated with any of the achievement measures.
After four weeks of treatment, the average daily methylpheni¬
date dose was 55 mg (range, 15 to 60 mg). After 12 weeks, the
average dose was 52 mg/day (range, 20 to 60 mg).
The drug effects on the psychological tests are presented in
Tables 5 and 6. The four-week results are discussed first; 12-week
After Four Weeks.—After four weeks, the only WISC subtest
with a mean effect significantly improved by methylphenidate
therapy was the information subtest (Table 5).
Scores on the reading tests (WRAT and Gray Oral Reading) did
not differ between the placebo and methylphenidate groups
(Table 6), though a slight trend in favor of the methylphenidate-
treated children on the WRAT reading scores was found (t
The WRAT arithmetic scores were greatly improved by methyl¬
phenidate therapy after four weeks (P < .001).
spelling scores were not affected by treatment.
Among the cognitive tests (Table 6), a number of significant
drug-related changes were observed after four weeks of treat¬
In keeping with other investigators' findings using samples
from different populations, the Porteus maze test IQ was
tremely sensitive to methylphenidate (P < .001). The qualitative
score, which was obtained by dividing the Porteus qualitative
score by the number of mazes completed, reflected drug-related
as well, at four weeks. The Visual Sequential
Memory Test, which, like the Porteus maze test, requires visual
(P< .005). The Visual Motor Integration Test also showed
notable drug effect (P < .005). The Draw-A-Person, Continuous
Performance, and Paired Associate Tests were not differentially
affected by the stimulant after four weeks.
After 12 Weeks.-The drug effect on the WISC was greater after
12 weeks of treatment (Table 5). Among the verbal tests of the
WISC, the similarities subtest scores showed a significant drug
effect. Information scale scores were no longer different between
The performance IQ showed a six-point increment attributable
to medication after 12 weeks of treatment (P < .025). The drug
effect is reflected in a four-point gain on the full-scale IQ. The
increment in performance IQ was due to significant gains in favor
of the drug-treated children on picture completion, block design,
Table 4.—Intercorrelations of Baseline Characteristics
Methylphenidate Subjects (N = 29)
t Wide-Range Achievement Test.
Table 5.—Baseline Covariate, Adjusted Posttreatment Means of WISC* Scores
* Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children.
f Placebo group:  = 32 at week 4, 31 at week 12; methylphenidate group:
% One-tailed test of significance.
and coding. These performance subtests deal with relatively
meaningless content. The two subtests that require synthesis of
familiar and meaningful stimuli (ie, picture arrangement and
object assembly) were unaffected by the medication.
The previously observed trend in favor of methylphenidate-
treated children on the WRAT reading scores was still present
after 12 weeks, but did not reach statistical significance (i
Strikingly, the notable advantage of the drug group in WRAT
no longer present. Whereas, after four
weeks of treatment, the drug group had a six-month superiority
over the placebo group, after 12 weeks, the drug group scored, on
the average, about two months above the placebo group (scores
after drug administration were corrected for baseline value). The
stimulant-treated children showed relatively few changes between
weeks 4 to 12. On the other hand, the placebo-treated children, who
had shown no change over the first four weeks, improved suffi¬
ciently between weeks 4 and 12 to reach a level of performance

A number of the cognitive test scores that were not altered by
methylphenidate therapy over a one-month span showed a drug
effect after three months (Table 6). Both the Paired Associate and
Continuous Performance test scores were significantly improved
by methylphenidate after 12 weeks.
The number of errors of commission were significantly reduced
by methylphenidate treatment. Errors of omission
affected by the stimulant. This would tend to indicate that
children responded less to irrelevant stimuli with active treat¬
ment, whereas the failure to recognize criteria stimuli was not
the medication. Unfortunately,
Performance Test we used was too easy; as a result, the baseline
scores were very high and allowed little opportunity for the
detection of improvement. Given this limitation, it is remarkable
that any drug effect was obtained.
Further, the Paired Associate and Continuous Performance
tests are administered via a teaching machine, which, during our
first year, broke down frequently; the number of subjects at retest
is, therefore, considerably depressed, thereby limiting the chance
of detecting treatment effects.

inequality of slopes may indicate a specific interaction between
treatment and baseline patient characteristics.

The ultimate goal of treatment of learning disability children is
to improve academic performance. To determine whether children
who improved on one measure of achievement did so in other
measures, the 12-week corrected change scores of the achievement
tests were intercorrelated. The TRGI was included
evaluate to what extent the teacher's impressionistic evaluation of
the child reflected quantified improvement. In addition, analyses
were performed to determine to what extent improvement on
on achievement tests was associated, and
whether consistency of improvement occurred across cognitive
tests. Since a general improvement factor was anticipated, one-
tailed tests of significance were selected.

Methylphenidate Group.-Eleven significant associations
found at  < .025 or better, out of a possible 110, between change
in psychological test scores and change in academic performance
(Table 11). Though the number is small, it exceeds chance expec¬
tation, especially since all but one of the relationships are in the
predicted direction. The salient findings are that improvement in
visual sequential memory is associated positively with teacher
ratings of reading improvement and with gains on the Gray Oral
Reading Test. Improving the ability to recall visual stimuli
organized in a set order seems to influence the capacity to read.

There was no pattern of change relationships observable in this
The operational definition of learning disability used in
this study was a two-year lag in reading achievement in
elementary school children, in spite of normal IQ. Others
have used the child's mental age, rather than IQ, to
evaluate his expected attainment in reading,
learning disabilities are defined by a discrepancy between
expected and actual reading levels. Since the latter defini¬
tion was not used in this study, results obtained cannot be
simply generalized to children who meet these different
criteria for learning disability. However, 72% of the chil¬
dren in this study were at least two years behind the
achievement level congruent with their mental age. The
mean deficit on the Gray Oral Reading Test was 2.95 years
(SD, 1.47) below the mean mental age.
The results, using a group of children selected exclu¬
sively for learning problems and excluding children with
hyperactivity or behavior disorders, confirm the findings
that stimulants are instrumental in improving perfor¬
mance in children. The tests found sensitive to drug effect
did not differ from those previously reported in mixed
diagnostic groups. Thus, our design allows us to affirm that
stimulant improvement of cognition is not secondary to the
drug's ameliorative impact on psychosocial functioning.
With regard to the primary goal of academic improve¬
ment using achievement tests and teachers' evaluations,
methylphenidate is not a useful agent under the conditions
of this study. This finding raises
validity of administering stimulants to improve learning,
academic achievement than cognitive tests.
The fact that teachers perceived greater improvement
among children from a higher SES treated with methyl¬
phenidate cannot be attributed to bias, since the opposite
relationship was found among placebo-treated children.
The greater placebo response of more deprived children
suggests that these children were reacting to attention
from the staff and the parent who made weekly visits to
the clinic with the child. It is possible that the children
from a higher SES with stable home environments had
already been given special attention at home via homework
assistance and other factors, prior to treatment initiation.
Therefore, whatever improvement attention could induce
in these children might have taken place already, before
There was no overall improvement factor among the
achievement tests for the drug-treated children. Our
results suggest that in studies of learning disabilities,
several measures should be used to sample a variety of
academic tasks. It cannot be assumed that one test is
sufficient if it correlates highly with other tests. The fact
that measures are highly related before treatment does not
predict that they will change together with treatment.
Our results suggest that visual processing mechanisms
are the most sensitive to stimulants in this population. If
so, it would be unlikely for academic functioning to be
meaningfully altered by methylphenidate therapy, since
verbal skills are crucial for adequate school performance.
expressive skills are improved in children with learning
disabilities, little hope of classroom change can be antic¬
ipated. This view is at variance with recent trends in the
field of learning disability where emphasis has been placed
on the training of deficient motor-perceptual skills, as a
means of facilitating reading aptitude. Our findings pose a
direct challenge to this view. Though a number of cognitive
tasks were improved, especially those involving visual
perceptual processes, these gains were not associated with
concurrent increments in reading performance.
Drug-induced gains in arithmetic computation followed
a different pattern. The WRAT arithmetic change scores
were positively correlated with improvement on several
visual-motor tasks. Improvement on these tasks, however,
was not associated with improvement on the WISC arith¬
This finding suggests that the composite of arithmetic
processes tapped by the WISC and WRAT may include
qualitative differences. The two tests
though highly significant, the association is weak; plainly,
the two tests measure more than the same function. The
unimproved WISC arithmetic subtest taps problem-solving
as well as computational skills: the child must both select
the proper operation and perform it orally; whereas, on the
WRAT, initially benefited by medication, the selection
process is absent. Mathematical comprehension is unneces¬
sary and the stimulus is processed visually, underscoring
the possible drug effect on visual perceptual functions.
One danger is to attribute too broad a role to the visual
mode in learning disability as demonstrated in this study,
since improvement did not lead to better reading perfor¬
mance. It might be that insufficient time had elapsed to
allow for the change in visual functions to affect more
complex tasks such as reading. However, this might not be
the case, since one-month trends for improvement in
reading on the WRAT did not increase after three months
Another possibility is that the learning capacity of the
children was significantly enhanced by the stimulant, but
in the absence of concurrent special education, the children
improved skills. All the children were attending school
throughout the experimental period. Yet it is conceivable
that methylphenidate significantly contributes to academ¬
ic performance only when used in combination with reme¬
dial instruction. For instance, the standard classroom
situation might not suffice if the children had developed a
negative attitude and did not apply themselves to their
work. We are currently testing this hypothesis.
The significant negative relationship between change in
WISC comprehension and WRAT spelling scores should
probably be attributed to chance factors. This is the only
finding opposite from the predicted direction.
The fact that a notable short-term, but no long-term
effect was found in arithmetic warns against the practice
of generalizing from results obtained in studies of a few
The data established that stimulants enhance certain
cognitive processes in children. This finding is compatible
with the theory that stimulants do not have a paradoxical
effect on children, at least with regard to cognitive pro¬
" Nonetheless, it is conceivable that stimulants do
regulatory mechanisms. This issue will be discussed in
another article dealing with the lack of behavioral activa¬
tion by methylphenidate in this sample.

were improved by dextroamphet-
amine therapy. However, our findings tend to show that
such improvements did not occur in children with learning
different from adults. More speculatively, learning-dis¬
abled children may have a specific defect in left hemis¬
treated with stimulants, their residual normal brain func¬
tions show an increment, but their damaged functional
capacities do not, leading to the apparent specificity of
drug effect on nonconceptual visual processing skills. To
resolve this question would require the study of stimulant
effects in normal, as well as learning-disabled children. If
the normal children had conceptual verbal improvement
similar to those reported in normal adults,10 the evidence
for qualitatively different neuropsychologic functioning in
learning disabilities would be conclusive. However, ethical
considerations raise questions about such investigations.
Nontherapeutic studies require a compelling social interest
As is true for "normals," the subjects in this study had no
significant behavior disorders, and in this sense, were not
"disturbed," but they had a manifest dysfunction. The
investigation of methylphenidate effects was justified by
the growing use of the medication in such patients,
practice that required study since it had no scientific basis.
